content.text: in CrewAI is a discrete unit of work assigned to a specific agent. Every task has a
(what a successful result looks like), and an

(who does the work). Tasks are the building blocks of a crew's workflow: they are executed in sequence or in parallel depending on the crew's process, and each task's output can be passed as context to the next one.

attrs.alt: CrewAI Task lifecycle: defined, assigned to agent, executed by agent, output validated against expected_output, result returned; context_from feeds prior output into later tasks

content.text: The three required fields on every

from crewai import Agent, Task
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini")

researcher = Agent(
    role="Research Analyst",
    goal="Find accurate information on any topic.",
    backstory="You are a meticulous researcher who cites sources.",
    llm=llm,
)

research_task = Task(
    description=(
        "Research the top three applications of transformer models in "
        "natural language processing as of 2024. For each application, "
        "provide a one-paragraph explanation and a real-world example."
    ),
    expected_output=(
        "A structured list of three transformer NLP applications, "
        "each with a paragraph explanation and a concrete example."
    ),
    agent=researcher,
)
What the agent must do — injected into the agent's prompt
Describes a successful result; used by the agent to self-evaluate
The Agent instance responsible for this task
Override the agent's tool list for this specific task
List of other Task objects whose output is injected as context
Path to write the task output to a file automatically
A Pydantic model class; CrewAI parses the output into it
True to parse and return the output as a JSON dict
A callable invoked with the TaskOutput when the task completes
True to pause and ask for human review before finalising
True to run this task concurrently with others

to inject the output of earlier tasks as context for a later one. The crew automatically includes those outputs in the agent's prompt so it can build on prior work without you needing to pass data manually.

from crewai import Agent, Task, Crew
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini")

researcher = Agent(role="Researcher", goal="Find facts.", backstory="Meticulous.", llm=llm)
writer = Agent(role="Writer", goal="Write clearly.", backstory="Expert writer.", llm=llm)

research_task = Task(
    description="Research the history and key features of the Rust programming language.",
    expected_output="A factual summary covering Rust's origin, ownership model, and key use cases.",
    agent=researcher,
)

writing_task = Task(
    description=(
        "Write a 300-word blog introduction about Rust, targeted at Python developers. "
        "Use the research provided in the context."
    ),
    expected_output="A 300-word blog intro paragraph about Rust for Python developers.",
    agent=writer,
    context=[research_task],   # inject researcher's output as context
)

crew = Crew(agents=[researcher, writer], tasks=[research_task, writing_task])
result = crew.kickoff()
print(result.raw)
to have the task's output automatically parsed into a typed Python object. This eliminates manual JSON parsing and gives you IDE autocompletion and type safety on the result.

from pydantic import BaseModel
from crewai import Agent, Task, Crew
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

class ArticleSummary(BaseModel):
    title: str
    key_points: list[str]
    word_count_estimate: int

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini")
analyst = Agent(
    role="Analyst",
    goal="Summarise articles into structured data.",
    backstory="You produce precise structured summaries.",
    llm=llm,
)

summary_task = Task(
    description=(
        "Summarise this article: 'Rust 2024 Edition introduces new async traits, "
        "improved ergonomics, and faster compile times.' "
        "Return a title, three key bullet points, and an estimated word count for a full article."
    ),
    expected_output="A structured summary with title, key_points list, and word_count_estimate.",
    agent=analyst,
    output_pydantic=ArticleSummary,
)

crew = Crew(agents=[analyst], tasks=[summary_task])
result = crew.kickoff()

# result.pydantic is an ArticleSummary instance
summary: ArticleSummary = result.pydantic
print(summary.title)
print(summary.key_points)
print(summary.word_count_estimate)
Writing Task Output to a File
to a file path and CrewAI will write the task's final output to that file automatically after execution. Useful for reports, generated code files, or any deliverable that needs to be persisted.

from crewai import Task

report_task = Task(
    description=(
        "Write a comprehensive report on the state of quantum computing in 2024, "
        "covering hardware milestones, algorithm advances, and near-term applications."
    ),
    expected_output="A structured 600-word report with headings and bullet points.",
    agent=researcher,
    output_file="reports/quantum_computing_2024.md",  # saved automatically
)

on a task to pause the crew after the agent produces its draft output and ask a human to review and optionally modify it before the crew continues. The console displays the draft and prompts for input.

from crewai import Task

review_task = Task(
    description="Draft a press release about the product launch.",
    expected_output="A professional 200-word press release.",
    agent=writer,
    human_input=True,   # pauses for human review before finalising
)

# During execution the console shows:
# === Agent Output ===
# [draft press release text]
# === Human Review ===
# Please provide feedback (press Enter to accept):

on tasks that can run in parallel. In a sequential crew process, tasks marked async are launched concurrently and their results are gathered before any dependent task begins. This reduces total wall-clock time when tasks do not depend on each other.

from crewai import Task

# These two tasks can run at the same time
research_task_a = Task(
    description="Research the history of Python.",
    expected_output="A brief history of Python.",
    agent=researcher,
    async_execution=True,   # launch concurrently
)
research_task_b = Task(
    description="Research the history of Rust.",
    expected_output="A brief history of Rust.",
    agent=researcher,
    async_execution=True,   # launch concurrently
)

# This task waits for both above to complete (uses both as context)
comparison_task = Task(
    description="Compare Python and Rust based on the provided research.",
    expected_output="A 200-word comparison of Python vs Rust.",
    agent=writer,
    context=[research_task_a, research_task_b],  # waits for both
    async_execution=False,
)
function to a task to run custom code after the task completes. The callback receives a
object with the raw text, Pydantic object (if set), and JSON (if set).

from crewai import Task
from crewai.tasks.task_output import TaskOutput

def on_research_done(output: TaskOutput):
    print(f"Task complete. Output length: {len(output.raw)} chars")
    if output.pydantic:
        print(f"Structured: {output.pydantic}")

research_task = Task(
    description="Research neural scaling laws.",
    expected_output="A factual summary of neural scaling laws.",
    agent=researcher,
    callback=on_research_done,
)
CrewAI allows tasks to run conditionally based on the output of a previous task. The
parameter accepts a callable that receives the previous task's output and returns a boolean.

from crewai import Task, Agent, Crew
from crewai.tasks.conditional_task import ConditionalTask

def needs_research(output):
    # Skip research step if summary is already comprehensive
    return len(output.raw) < 500

researcher = Agent(role="Researcher", goal="Find missing details", backstory="...")
research_task = ConditionalTask(
    description="Research additional context for the topic",
    expected_output="Supporting facts and references",
    agent=researcher,
    condition=needs_research,
)

summariser = Agent(role="Summariser", goal="Write final summary", backstory="...")
summary_task = Task(
    description="Write the final summary using all gathered information",
    expected_output="A concise two-paragraph summary",
    agent=summariser,
    context=[research_task],
)

crew = Crew(agents=[researcher, summariser], tasks=[research_task, summary_task])
result = crew.kickoff()
ConditionalTask is available in CrewAI ≥ 0.63. The condition function receives the
from the immediately preceding task in the pipeline.
Task guardrails let you validate and transform agent output before it is passed downstream. Set
on failure. CrewAI retries the task automatically when a guardrail fails.

import re
from crewai import Task

def validate_json_output(result):
    """Ensure the agent returned parseable JSON."""
    try:
        import json
        parsed = json.loads(result.raw)
        return True, parsed
    except json.JSONDecodeError as e:
        return False, f"Output is not valid JSON: {e}"

extraction_task = Task(
    description="Extract the key entities from the document as JSON",
    expected_output="A JSON object with keys: people, places, events",
    agent=extractor_agent,
    guardrail=validate_json_output,
)
, CrewAI re-runs the task up to
times (default 2). Each retry passes the error message back to the agent so it can self-correct.
can assign sub-tasks to other agents in the crew. For fine-grained control over which tasks can delegate, use the

researcher = Agent(
    role="Lead Researcher",
    goal="Gather comprehensive data",
    backstory="...",
    allow_delegation=True,   # can spawn sub-tasks
)

analyst = Agent(
    role="Data Analyst",
    goal="Interpret gathered data",
    backstory="...",
    allow_delegation=False,  # stays focused on its own task
)

research_task = Task(
    description="Gather market data for Q3 2024",
    expected_output="Structured data table with sources",
    agent=researcher,
)
analysis_task = Task(
    description="Identify the top three trends from the gathered data",
    expected_output="Bullet list of trends with supporting figures",
    agent=analyst,
    context=[research_task],
)
type provides multiple views of the same result:
The agent's response as plain text
Validated Pydantic model if output_pydantic was set
Parsed dictionary if output_json was set
Role of the agent that produced this output

crew = Crew(agents=[...], tasks=[research_task, analysis_task])
result = crew.kickoff()

# Access individual task outputs
print(research_task.output.raw)          # full text
print(analysis_task.output.pydantic)     # Pydantic model if configured
print(analysis_task.output.agent)        # "Data Analyst"

# The final CrewOutput wraps all task outputs
print(result.raw)          # last task's raw text
print(result.tasks_output) # list of all TaskOutput objects
(default), tasks execute in list order. Switch to
to unlock parallel execution — the manager agent decides which tasks to dispatch concurrently.

from crewai import Crew, Process
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

crew = Crew(
    agents=[scraper, analyser, writer],
    tasks=[scrape_task, analyse_task, write_task],
    process=Process.hierarchical,
    manager_llm=ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o"),
    verbose=True,
)
result = crew.kickoff()
The manager agent is synthetic — it is created automatically from
and never appears in your agents list. It orchestrates task dispatch and collects results, then hands final output back to the caller.
: 'A good analysis' gives the agent no target. Be specific: 'A bullet list of exactly five findings with supporting data, formatted as markdown.'
get stale or hallucinated data. Always wire the dependency explicitly.
: Complex nested models often cause parse failures on the first attempt. Keep output schemas flat for reliability; add nesting only when the downstream code requires it.
: Blocking on human input inside an automated pipeline will hang indefinitely. Guard with environment checks or use a custom
after setting up async tasks and results are never awaited. Make sure the crew gathers all futures before returning.
A Task has three required fields: description (what to do), expected_output (what success looks like), and agent (who does the work).
Use context=[task1, task2] to inject prior task outputs as context for a later task — the agent sees those outputs in its prompt.
output_pydantic parses the task output into a typed Pydantic model; output_json returns a dict; output_file writes to disk.
human_input=True pauses the crew after the agent drafts output and prompts a human to review before continuing.
async_execution=True launches the task concurrently with other async tasks; context dependencies still synchronise correctly.
callback receives a TaskOutput after each task completes — useful for logging, metrics, or side effects.
